interactions.teaches: Allows users to rotate the view slightly along the horizontal axis to understand the perspective better while maintaining the 2D characteristics of the original figure.
Hovering over any line or plane displays a tooltip explaining its role in perspective projection, such as why lines converge at the vanishing point.
Clicking on the vanishing point reveals intersection lines with multiple planes, illustrating how all lines sharing the same angle in 3D space converge at a single point in a 2D perspective.

demo_steps.title: Introduction to vanishing point
demo_steps.narration: Here's a set of parallel lines converging at a vanishing point on the horizon. This illustrates that objects parallel to each other appear to meet at the horizon when extended to infinity.

demo_steps.title: Exploring planes and intersections
demo_steps.narration: Click the vanishing point to see how lines intersect with different planes. Each plane represents a different slice of the 3D space showing the trajectory of parallel lines.

demo_steps.title: Axes and perspective
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the view slightly to observe the alignment and orientation of the X, Y, and Z axes without distorting the perspective view seen originally. This helps in understanding the viewpoint scale.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-on view centered on the horizon line, ensuring minimal distortion. The vanishing point should remain in the center as seen in the original, keeping lines and planes aligned to simulate a 2D perspective projection.

plan.notes: Maintain orthographic rendering to preserve line convergence regardless of slight rotations, ensuring axis and plane annotations remain readable. Adjust the animation curve gently to avoid abrupt changes while exploring hidden states.

plan.contextChunk:
$t$ goes to infinity, the line converges to a finite point called the
vanishing point: $$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{p}_t =
\left [
\begin{array}{c}
    f \dfrac{D_X}{D_Z}\\[12pt]
    f \dfrac{D_Y}{D_Z}
    \end{array}
    \right ]\end{aligned}$${#eq-vanishingpoint} The vanishing point,
$\mathbf{v} = [v_x, v_y]^\mathsf{T}$, only depends on the direction
vector, $\mathbf{D}$, thus all parallel lines in 3D project into
non-parallel 2D images that converge to the same vanishing point in the
image. If $D_Z = 0$ then the 3D line is parallel to the camera plane and
the vanishing point is in infinite.

@fig-vanishing_lines_and_planes shows a set of parallel 3D lines and
their 2D image projections. Each group of parallel lines converges to a
different vanishing point. Lines that are parallel to the camera plane
remain parallel as they converge to a vanishing point in infinity.

![Parallel 3D lines converge to the same vanishing point. (a) The red lines are parallel to the optical axis and converge to a vanishing point in the center. (b) Two sets of parallel lines contained in an horizontal plane, parallel to the optical axis. Their vanishing points are on an horizontal image line.](figures/single_view_3d/vanishing_lines_and_planes.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_lines_and_planes}

All the sets of parallel lines inside a plane converge to a set of
co-linear vanishing points. These points lie along the plane's horizon
line.

Vanishing points are very useful to recover the missing depth of the
scene as we will see later. The human visual system also makes use of
vanishing points for 3D perception. Take, for example, the *leaning
tower visual illusion* @Kingdom2017 as depicted in @fig-LeaningTower1.
Here, a pair of identical images of the Leaning Tower of Pisa are placed
side by side. Interestingly, despite being identical, they create an
illusion of leaning with different angles. The image on the right
appears to have a greater tilt than the one on the left. One explanation
of this illusion is that the lines of each tower converge at a different
vanishing point. If both towers were parallel in 3D, all the lines would

elements: set of parallel red 3D lines converging to the central vanishing point
different sets of parallel cyan 3D lines converging to different vanishing points
horizontal plane containing sets of parallel lines

interactions.teaches: Exploring the scene from different angles to understand the convergence of lines toward vanishing points.
Hover to highlight lines and see their projected vanishing points.

interactions.label: Show/Hide Plane
interactions.teaches: Toggle the visibility of the horizontal plane to see how it aids in visualizing vanishing points and horizon lines.

demo_steps.title: Initial scene setup
demo_steps.narration: Here we see two sets of parallel lines, some are parallel to the optical axis, while others lie in a horizontal plane. Notice how they converge at distinct vanishing points on the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Vanishing Points on Horizon Line
demo_steps.narration: Observe how the cyan lines converge to vanishing points that align along a horizontal horizon line in the image plane. This demonstrates the nature of parallel lines in the 3D plane converging to co-linear vanishing points.

demo_steps.title: Parallel to Optical Axis
demo_steps.narration: When focusing on the red lines, which are parallel to the optical axis, they converge to a point at the center of the image plane, illustrating how direction impacts the position of the vanishing point.

plan.camera_suggestion: Align the camera to closely match the frontal view in the original image, ensuring a perspective that captures the convergence of lines towards the vanishing points, with the horizon line appearing level.

inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

plan.notes: Use thin solid lines for 3D perceived lines and ensure dashed lines for horizon delineation. Accurate alignment of green axes arrows as per source image is crucial.
